title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
After spending significant time trying to run the original 3D-RCAN code (documented in my reproducibility challenges post), I decided to rewrite the entire network from scratch in modern PyTorch.
This approach has several advantages: - PyTorch runs natively on Apple M3 via Metal Performance Shaders (MPS) - Modern PyTorch is actively maintained and well documented - Building from scratch forces a deeper understanding of the architecture - No dependency on 5-year-old Keras/TensorFlow code

title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
After spending significant time trying to run the original 3D-RCAN code (documented in my reproducibility challenges post), I decided to rewrite the entire network from scratch in modern PyTorch.
This approach has several advantages: - PyTorch runs natively on Apple M3 via Metal Performance Shaders (MPS) - Modern PyTorch is actively maintained and well documented - Building from scratch forces a deeper understanding of the architecture - No dependency on 5-year-old Keras/TensorFlow code

title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
The Architecture
The 3D-RCAN network is built from four components:

Channel Attention
Learns which feature channels matter most:
class ChannelAttention(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):
        z = self.gap(x).view(x.size(0), -1)
        s = self.fc(z).view(x.size(0), -1, 1, 1, 1)
        return x * s  # scale each channel by learned weight


RCAB (Residual Channel Attention Block)
Core processing unit with skip connection:
class RCAB(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):
        residual = x
        out = self.relu(self.conv1(x))
        out = self.conv2(out)
        out = self.ca(out)
        return out + residual  # skip connection


Full RCAN3D Network
class RCAN3D(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):
        mean, std = x.mean(), x.std() + 1e-8
        x = (x - mean) / std          # normalize
        head = self.head(x)            # feature extraction
        body = self.body(head)         # 3 residual groups
        out = self.tail(body + head)   # global skip connection
        return out * std + mean        # denormalize

title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
Running on Apple M3
PyTorch supports M3 via Metal Performance Shaders (MPS):
Using device: mps
Total parameters: 154,035
Model works!

title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
Training on Real Data
I trained the model on 5,600 paired image crops of aberrated and clean C. elegans worm embryo images provided by the authors’ lab.
Training configuration: - Epochs: 5 - Batch size: 2 - Loss function: MAE (same as original paper) - Optimizer: Adam, lr=1e-4 - Crop size: 16 × 128 × 128

Training Loss Curve



Training Loss


The loss decreased consistently from 11.3 → 10.49 across 5 epochs, confirming the model is learning. The curve is still declining, meaning more training epochs would further improve results.

title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
Exploring the Data
First I visualized all 45 Z-slices to find the best region:



All Slices


The worm embryo is most visible in slices 18-35, where the cell membrane network is clearly visible.

title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
Aberration Correction Results
Applying the trained model to slice 26 — the middle of the embryo:



Before and After


Left (Original): Soft, blurry cell membranes with diffuse signal
Right (Trained Model): Sharp, crisp cell membrane boundaries with clearly defined individual cells
The model successfully learned to correct optical aberrations and enhance the visibility of biological structures in the C. elegans embryo!

title: Reimplementing 3D-RCAN in Modern PyTorch
text:
Conclusions
With only 5 epochs and a smaller model, the PyTorch rewrite already shows meaningful aberration correction on real C. elegans microscopy data. The original paper trained for 200 epochs — with more training time the results would approach the paper’s quality.
This demonstrates that the 3D-RCAN architecture can be successfully reimplemented in modern frameworks and run on consumer hardware like the Apple M3.

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text: The 3D-RCAN network might seem like a black box, but it is built from three elegant mathematical ideas that connect directly to linear algebra. In this post I break down each one.

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text: The 3D-RCAN network might seem like a black box, but it is built from three elegant mathematical ideas that connect directly to linear algebra. In this post I break down each one.

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text:
1. Convolutions — Sliding Matrix Multiplication
A 3D convolution is the core operation in this network. Conceptually, we slide a small matrix called a kernel \(W\) over the input volume \(x\) and compute a weighted sum at each position:
\[y[i,j,k] = \sum_{p,q,r} W[p,q,r] \cdot x[i+p, j+q, k+r]\]
Where: - \(x\) is the input 3D image (a tensor of shape \(Z \times Y \times X\)) - \(W\) is the learned kernel (e.g. \(3 \times 3 \times 3\)) - \(y\) is the output feature map
Think of it as a dot product between the kernel and every local patch of the image. The network learns the values of \(W\) during training.
In matrix notation for a single patch:
\[y = W \cdot x_{patch} + b\]
Where \(b\) is a bias term. This is pure linear algebra.

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text:
2. Residual Learning — Learning the Difference
A key innovation in RCAN is residual learning. Instead of learning the full mapping from input to output directly, the network learns the residual — the difference between input and desired output.
A standard network learns: \[y = F(x)\]
A residual network learns: \[y = F(x) + x\]
The \(+x\) term is called a skip connection. It passes the original input directly to the output and adds it to the learned transformation.
Why does this help? Because learning \(F(x) = 0\) (no change needed) is much easier than learning \(F(x) = x\) (identity mapping). The network only needs to learn corrections, not the full image.
In the 3D-RCAN architecture this is applied hierarchically — residuals within residuals, called Residual-in-Residual structure:
\[y_{group} = F_{group}(x) + x\] \[y_{block} = F_{block}(x) + x\]

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text:
3. Channel Attention — Adaptive Feature Weighting
Not all feature channels are equally important. The channel attention mechanism learns to weight each channel adaptively.
For a feature map with \(C\) channels, we first compute the global average of each channel (called Global Average Pooling):
\[z_c = \frac{1}{Z \cdot Y \cdot X} \sum_{i,j,k} x_c[i,j,k]\]
This gives us a vector \(z \in \mathbb{R}^C\) — one number per channel summarizing its overall activation.
Then we pass it through two small linear layers with a bottleneck:
\[s = \sigma\left(W_2 \cdot \text{ReLU}(W_1 \cdot z)\right)\]
Where: - \(W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{C/r \times C}\) reduces dimensions (r is reduction ratio) - \(W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C/r}\) restores dimensions - \(\sigma\) is the sigmoid function (outputs values between 0 and 1)
Finally we multiply each channel by its learned weight:
\[\hat{x}_c = s_c \cdot x_c\]
This tells the network: pay more attention to channels that contain useful information, and suppress channels that don’t.

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
section: 4. Putting It All Together: The RCAB Block
text:
4. Putting It All Together: The RCAB Block
A Residual Channel Attention Block (RCAB) combines all three ideas:
\[\text{RCAB}(x) = x + \text{ChannelAttention}(\text{Conv}(\text{ReLU}(\text{Conv}(x))))\]
In words: 1. Apply two convolutions with ReLU activation 2. Apply channel attention to reweight features 3. Add the original input back (residual connection)
The full 3D-RCAN stacks multiple RCABs into Residual Groups, and multiple Residual Groups into the full network.

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text:
5. Training: How the Network Learns
The network is trained on pairs of images: - Input: Aberrated microscopy image \(\hat{x}\) - Target: Clean ground truth image \(x^*\)
It minimizes the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) loss:
\[\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} |f(\hat{x}_i) - x^*_i|\]
Where \(f\) is the network and \(N\) is the number of pixels. The network updates its weights using gradient descent:
\[W \leftarrow W - \alpha \nabla_W \mathcal{L}\]
Where \(\alpha\) is the learning rate. This is the chain rule of calculus applied to matrices — exactly what you study in multivariable calculus and linear algebra!

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text:
Connection to Your Classes



Concept
Class




Matrix multiplication (convolutions)
Linear Algebra


Gradient descent, chain rule
Multivariable Calculus


Dot products, vector spaces
Linear Algebra


Eigenvalues (feature channels)
Linear Algebra


Loss minimization
Calculus

title: The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention
text:
Summary
3D-RCAN is elegant because it builds a powerful image restoration system from simple mathematical operations you already know: - Convolution = sliding dot products - Residual learning = addition of matrices - Channel attention = learned scaling of vectors - Training = gradient descent on a loss function
The “deep learning” part is really just applying these operations millions of times with learned parameters.

title: Reproducing Deep Learning-Based Aberration Compensation for Fluorescence Microscopy
text: For my senior project, I am reproducing the work from the paper “Deep learning-based aberration compensation improves contrast and resolution in fluorescence microscopy” by Min Guo et al. (Nature Communications, 2024).

title: Reproducing Deep Learning-Based Aberration Compensation for Fluorescence Microscopy
text: For my senior project, I am reproducing the work from the paper “Deep learning-based aberration compensation improves contrast and resolution in fluorescence microscopy” by Min Guo et al. (Nature Communications, 2024).

title: Reproducing Deep Learning-Based Aberration Compensation for Fluorescence Microscopy
text:
What is the paper about?
The paper introduces a deep learning pipeline called DeAbe+ that improves the quality of fluorescence microscopy images. When scientists image biological samples like worm embryos, the images get distorted and blurry — especially deep inside thick tissue. The authors trained neural networks to fix these distortions automatically without needing special hardware.

title: Reproducing Deep Learning-Based Aberration Compensation for Fluorescence Microscopy
text:
The Pipeline
The method works in 3 steps:

De-aberration (Step 1): Fixes optical distortions using a 3D-RCAN network
Deconvolution (Step 2): Sharpens the image using another 3D-RCAN network

Isotropic Enhancement (Step 3): Improves resolution in all directions using a CARE network

title: Reproducing Deep Learning-Based Aberration Compensation for Fluorescence Microscopy
text:
My Goals

Download and explore the provided dataset (C. elegans worm embryo images)
Run the pre-trained models on the test data
Reproduce the key figures from the paper
Document every step of the process

title: Reproducing Deep Learning-Based Aberration Compensation for Fluorescence Microscopy
text:
Data
The data is publicly available at Zenodo and was also kindly shared directly by the authors’ lab.

title: Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026
text:
One of the most important, and often overlooked, aspects of science is reproducibility: the ability for other researchers to run the same code and get the same results. This post documents the real challenges I encountered trying to run the 3D-RCAN pipeline from Guo et al. (2024) on modern hardware and software in 2026.
Spoiler: it did not work out of the box.

title: Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026
text:
One of the most important, and often overlooked, aspects of science is reproducibility: the ability for other researchers to run the same code and get the same results. This post documents the real challenges I encountered trying to run the 3D-RCAN pipeline from Guo et al. (2024) on modern hardware and software in 2026.
Spoiler: it did not work out of the box.

title: Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026
text:
My Environment

Computer: MacBook Air M3 (Apple Silicon), 16GB RAM
OS: macOS Sequoia 15.6
Python: 3.12 (system), 3.10 (virtual environment)
Goal: Run the pre-trained de-aberration model on C. elegans test images

title: Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026
text:
What the Code Requires
The 3D-RCAN code was written in 2021 and requires:



Dependency
Required Version
Latest Version (2026)




Python
3.7
3.13


TensorFlow
1.13
2.21


Keras
2.2.4
3.x


CUDA
10.0
12.x


GPU
NVIDIA only
—

title: Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026
text:
The Compatibility Timeline

Attempt 1 — Run directly on Mac M3
Running apply.py with system Python 3.12 and TensorFlow 2.21 immediately failed:
AttributeError: module 'tensorflow' has no attribute 'logging'
Root cause: tf.logging was removed in TensorFlow 2.x.
Fix: Replace with tf.compat.v1.logging


Attempt 2 — Keras serialization error
After fixing the logging issue:
TypeError: Could not locate class 'Model'
Root cause: Keras 3.x completely changed its model serialization format.
Fix attempted: Replace model_from_config() with model_from_json() — partially worked but hit further issues.


Attempt 3 — Apple Silicon threading crash
libc++abi: terminating due to uncaught exception of type 
std::__1::system_error: mutex lock failed
Root cause: TensorFlow’s threading model conflicts with macOS on M3.
Fix: Set OBJC_DISABLE_INITIALIZE_FORK_SAFETY=YES


Attempt 4 — Google Colab
Moved to Google Colab hoping their NVIDIA GPUs would help:
ERROR: No matching distribution found for tensorflow-gpu==1.13.1
Root cause: TensorFlow 1.x is no longer available on any platform.


Attempt 5 — Python 3.10 + tensorflow-macos
Created a fresh virtual environment with Python 3.10 and tensorflow-macos==2.12.0 (the last version before Keras 3 broke everything):
Metal device set to: Apple M3
systemMemory: 16.00 GB
Loading model from RCAN_model/weights_169_0.05605552.hdf5
Progress! The model file was found and Metal GPU detected. But then:
ValueError: bad marshal data (unknown type code)
Root cause: The model contains Lambda layers whose bytecode was compiled with Python 3.7. Python’s marshal module is not compatible across versions, so the bytecode cannot be loaded in Python 3.10.

title: Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026
text:
Why This Matters
This experience highlights software rot, a real and growing problem in computational science. Code that worked perfectly in 2021 may not run at all in 2026 due to:

Dependency deprecation
Breaking API changes between major versions

Hardware architecture evolution (x86 → Apple Silicon)
Cloud platform updates removing old package versions

title: Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026
text:
Next Steps

Continue working with the original authors to get a working environment
Rewrite the inference code in modern PyTorch which runs natively on Mac M3
Compare results to the paper’s published figures

title: About
text:
I’m a senior student working on reproducing deep learning-based aberration compensation for fluorescence microscopy as my senior project.
This blog documents my journey through the paper by Min Guo et al. (2024), including data exploration, model training, and results.
